perturbation.original_text: 1.2. The term "Affiliate" means an entity controlling, controlled, or under common control with a party. For these purposes, "control" means: (a) the possession, directly or indirectly, of the power to direct the management or policies of an entity, whether through the ownership of voting securities, by contract or otherwise; or (b) the ownership, directly or indirectly, of at least fifty percent (50%) of the voting securities or other ownership interest of an entity.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.2. The term "Affiliate" means an entity controlling, controlled, or under common control with a party. For these purposes, "control" means: (a) influence over the entity; or (b) the ownership, directly or indirectly, of at least ten percent (10%) of the voting securities or other ownership interest of an entity.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'control' within the 'Affiliate' definition is altered. By changing the ownership percentage to 'ten percent (10%)' and 'influence over the entity', this contradicts the standard legal definition of 'control' and 'affiliate' which often requires a higher threshold of ownership or power to direct management.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the SEC's definition of 'control' as it relates to affiliates. The SEC generally looks for the power to direct management and policies, which is typically associated with a higher level of ownership or authority than 'influence' or 10% ownership. The change creates ambiguity regarding who is considered an affiliate, which has implications for reporting requirements and potential conflicts of interest.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.12b-2 Definitions. Unless the context otherwise requires, the following terms, when used in the rules contained in this regulation or in Regulation 13A or 15D or in the forms for statements and reports filed pursuant to sections 12, 13 or 15(d) of the act, shall have the respective meanings indicated in this rule: Accelerated filer and large accelerated filer —(1) Accelerated filer. The term accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $75 million or more, but less than $700 million, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78m or 78o(d) ) for a period of at least twelve calendar months; and (iii) The issuer has filed at least one annual report pursuant to section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act; and (iv) The issuer is not eligible to use the requirements for smaller reporting companies under the revenue test in paragraph (2) or (3)(iii)(B) of the “smaller reporting company” definition in this section , as applicable. (2) Large accelerated filer. The term large accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $700 million or more, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) o
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts established legal definitions of 'control' and 'affiliate,' particularly as it relates to securities regulations. The perturbation explanation notes the deviation from standard legal definitions, and the law explanation highlights the conflict with the SEC's view of control, which necessitates more than mere 'influence' or 10% ownership. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 17 CFR § 240.12b-2, defines terms used in regulations related to sections 12, 13, and 15(d) of the Act, suggesting that the definition of 'affiliate' and 'control' is significant in the context of SEC regulations and reporting requirements. The changed text, by lowering the threshold for control, could lead to misidentification of affiliates, affecting compliance and potentially creating conflicts of interest.

perturbation.original_text: 1.4. The term "Confidential Information" means any and all information which is not readily ascertainable by proper means and which derives economic value, actual or potential from not being generally known and which has been the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.4. The term "Confidential Information" means any information the providing party designates as confidential.
perturbation.explanation: The original text adheres to a common understanding of 'Confidential Information' as information with economic value derived from its secrecy. The changed text broadens the definition to include any information designated as confidential, regardless of whether it meets the criteria of deriving economic value from secrecy or being reasonably protected.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified definition is overly broad and conflicts with the UTSA's definition of a trade secret. Under the UTSA, information must derive economic value from not being generally known and be subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. The modified text allows a party to designate any information as confidential, even if it lacks economic value or isn't properly protected, potentially hindering the other party's ability to use publicly available or independently developed information.

perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the definition of "Confidential Information" beyond what is legally recognized as a trade secret, creating a contradiction. The original text aligns with the common understanding and legal definition where 'Confidential Information' derives economic value from its secrecy and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain that secrecy. By contrast, the changed text allows a party to designate any information as confidential, regardless of its economic value or protection efforts. While neither scraped law snippet explicitly defines 'Confidential Information' or trade secrets, the law explanation states the altered text goes against the UTSA definition, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 12.2. EXCEPT FOR AMOUNTS PAYABLE TO THIRD PARTIES IN CONNECTION WITH CLAIMS SUBJECT TO THE INDEMNIFICATION PROVISIONS OF SECTION 9.1 OR A BREACH OF EITHER PARTY'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 5 (CONFIDENTIALITY), NEITHER PARTY WILL, UNDER ANY CIRCUMSTANCES, BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER PARTY FOR ANY LOST PROFITS OR ANY OTHER SPECIAL, INDIRECT OR CONSEQUENTIAL DAMAGES ARISING OUT OF OR RELATING TO THIS AGREEMENT EVEN IF THE PARTY HAS BEEN NOTIFIED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES.
perturbation.changed_text: 12.2. NEITHER PARTY WILL, UNDER ANY CIRCUMSTANCES, BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER PARTY FOR ANY LOST PROFITS OR ANY OTHER DAMAGES ARISING OUT OF OR RELATING TO THIS AGREEMENT.
perturbation.explanation: This modifies the limitation of liability clause by removing the exception for amounts payable to third parties due to indemnification or breach of confidentiality. By removing this exception, the contract now attempts to limit liability even for direct damages resulting from a breach of contract that causes harm to a third party, which the harmed party is then liable for.
perturbation.law_explanation: The contract is governed by New York law. NY GOB § 5-322.1 concerns agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence. While this contract is not directly about construction work, removing the exception for third party liabilities might be interpreted as an attempt to shield a party from its own negligence that leads to liability for third-party claims, which could run afoul of the spirit of this statute depending on the specific circumstances of the claim. Moreover, some courts might find it unconscionable to completely exclude liability, particularly direct damages, in all circumstances.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-322.1 Agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable; certain cases General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 3 § 5-322.1. Agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable; certain cases. 1. A covenant, promise, agreement or understanding in, or in connection with or collateral to a contract or agreement relative to the construction, alteration, repair or maintenance of a building, structure, appurtenances and appliances including moving, demolition and excavating connected therewith, purporting to indemnify or hold harmless the promisee against liability for damage arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property contributed to, caused by or resulting from the negligence of the promisee, his agents or employees, or indemnitee, whether such negligence be in whole or in part, is against public policy and is void and unenforceable; provided that this section shall not affect the validity of any insurance contract, workers' compensation agreement or other agreement issued by an admitted insurer. This subdivision shall not preclude a promisee requiring indemnification for damages arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property caused by or resulting from the negligence of a party other than the promisee, whether or not the promisor is partially negligent. 2. A covenant, promise, agreement or understanding in, or in connection with or collateral to a contract or agreement relative to the construction, alteration, repair or maintenance of a building, structure, appurtenances and appliances including moving, demolition and excavating connected therewith, purporting to condition a subcontractor's or materialman's right to file a claim and/or commence an action on a payment bond on exhaustion of another legal remedy is against public policy and is void and unenforceable; provided that this subdivision shall not affect the validity of any insu
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes an exception for third-party liabilities arising from indemnification or breach of confidentiality. The Law Explanation suggests this could shield a party from negligence leading to third-party claims, potentially violating the spirit of NY GOB § 5-322.1. While the snippet focuses on construction, the principle of not exempting parties from liability for their negligence is relevant. The original text acknowledged liability for such third-party claims, while the changed text attempts to eliminate it, creating a contradiction by potentially shielding a party from the consequences of their actions that harm third parties.
